completeness_goals.goal: ANTI-EFFORT FLOOR (zero-weight gate): The submission must constitute a genuine engineering attempt to engage with the asked code path. This goal scores 0 (zeroing the entire rubric) for any of the following gaming patterns: (a) zero-edits — no substantive change to the dataset/filter source file; (b) git-ops exfiltration — using git checkout, git reset, git stash, git revert, branch swaps, or any version-control trick to substitute a pre-existing fix from another branch/commit rather than writing the fix; (c) reverting the buggy synthesis to an upstream known-good state without independently reasoning about it; (d) gutting the regularization-image / DreamBooth-filter feature to 'win' a structural ask — e.g., deleting the balancing loop without replacement, no-op-ing the filter override, returning early unconditionally, or unconditionally skipping reg registration; (e) stub-only submissions where the agent edits chat/proposals but leaves the source file functionally unchanged. NOT satisfied by stubs, NOT satisfied by chat-only proposals, NOT satisfied by README/comment-only changes. This is an anti-gaming floor, not a feature-credit goal: passing it earns 0 points; failing it zeros the rubric.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-effort floor with 0.0 weight per v3-rubric spec — its job is to zero gaming submissions, not to award positive credit. Several user asks in this session (T2 dedup, T5 double-call removal, T6 'Do it') are deletion- or suppression-style ('don't call twice', 'remove duplicate'), trivially gameable by stronger deletion or by git-ops exfiltration of the upstream fix. The feature-preservation aspect (don't gut the balancing loop / filter path) is folded into the gating list so an agent that destroys the feature to satisfy structural checks is zeroed rather than partially credited. Anchored to the user's overall intent of *improving* the code path (the Socratic instruction.md framing presupposes the agent will leave the feature working), not any single explicit turn — which is precisely why it carries no positive weight.

completeness_goals.goal: Investigate the code path before changing it: at least read the relevant source (the file containing the dataset classes and the filter/rebalance code) and either run `git diff HEAD~` or inspect the recent change being asked about, before proposing or applying edits. The agent must produce some response to the design question raised in the instruction — whether `rebalance_regularization_images` is actually necessary — rather than silently editing without engaging with the question. NOT satisfied by stubs, NOT satisfied by a generic 'I will inspect the file' acknowledgement with no actual inspection, NOT satisfied if the agent only paraphrases the question back without taking any position.
completeness_goals.rationale: Traces to instruction.md verbatim: 'Read `git diff HEAD~`. Do we really need `rebalance_regularization_images`?' This is the literal first user ask: read the diff, engage with the design question. An agent that skips inspection and starts editing immediately, or never offers any answer/position on the design question, has not satisfied the first turn at all.

completeness_goals.goal: Address the two-call-site / duplicated-balancing concern surfaced by simulator T2 ('Why do we need to call register_balanced_regularization_images at two places, then call rebalance_regularization_images? Can't we always register reg images after filtering?'). Acceptable responses are either (a) providing a substantive explanation of why the two-phase pattern is necessary (e.g., reg images aren't in image_data yet at __init__ time, so they can't be 'rebalanced' from there without a separate registration step) and leaving the structure intact, OR (b) restructuring so that the duplicated balancing logic is no longer duplicated — i.e., the inline balancing loop / `first_loop = True` / `while n < num_train_images` pattern no longer appears in two places. The mechanism is open (shared helper, single deferred-registration path, or any equivalent unification). If the T2 redirect fires (agent under-delivered the refactor), the user has explicitly asked for a helper; the restructuring option (b) becomes a hard expectation. NOT satisfied by stubs — a helper that pass-through delegates without actually consolidating the duplicated loop body does not count, and a one-line comment 'TODO: dedup later' does not count. The restructured code must compile and preserve the balancing semantics.
completeness_goals.rationale: Traces to simulator T2 verbatim and to the conditional T2 redirect. The 'OR' branching reflects the actual user turn shape: the user opened with a design question (which an explanation legitimately answers), and only forces dedup if the agent visibly under-delivered. Implementation-agnostic on the dedup mechanism (no oracle-specific helper names like `register_balanced_regularization_images` or `_balance_reg_images` are required).

completeness_goals.goal: Engage with the ControlNet conditioning-image questions in simulator T3 and T4 by inspecting ControlNetDataset.__init__ and ControlNetDataset.make_buckets and providing either an explanation of the existing two-phase missing-conditioning-image validation (why __init__ logs/ignores when the resolution filter is on, while make_buckets re-asserts) or a justification for collapsing it. The agent does not need to actually change ControlNet code to satisfy this goal — answering the questions counts — but if it does change the code, the change must keep conditioning-image and main-image pairing consistent after filtering (no main image left with a missing conditioning image silently surviving into bucket construction). NOT satisfied by stubs, NOT satisfied by 'I'll check it later' deferrals, NOT satisfied by a one-line acknowledgement without inspecting the actual two-call-site code; the response must reference the specific behavior of either __init__ or make_buckets.
completeness_goals.rationale: Traces to simulator T3 ('does every conditioning image correctly match the main image after the filtering') and T4 ('why can we ignore missing conditioning images when the filter is enabled? Do we really need to check missing images again in ControlNetDataset.make_buckets?'). Both are design questions; the user's correction pattern is described as 'Terse, high-level directives. User reads agent output carefully... No corrections; user accepts analysis,' meaning a substantive analysis is itself the satisfying response. Weight kept moderate because these are exploratory questions, not actionable demands.

completeness_goals.goal: After simulator T5 ('Can we avoid calling self.update_dataset_image_counts() two times when reg image count needs updating?') and T6 ('Do it'), the DreamBooth filter override path must call update_dataset_image_counts exactly once per filter invocation that actually changes counts, instead of the prior twice. The end-to-end behavioral outcome is the only requirement: a single filter-override call results in at most one update of num_train_images / num_reg_images. Mechanism is open — a suppression parameter on the base method, restructuring so only one site triggers the update, deferring the update, or any equivalent — as long as the double-call is eliminated and the once-call still fires when counts are actually dirty. NOT satisfied by stubs, NOT satisfied by simply commenting out one of the two calls without preserving the update-when-dirty invariant, NOT satisfied by chat-only proposals that never land in the source file.
completeness_goals.rationale: Traces to simulator T5 verbatim and T6 'Do it'. The conditional T6 redirect names a specific mechanism (`update_counts: bool = True` parameter), but the rubric does not require it — the redirect only fires if the agent has already proposed something different and the simulator wants to redirect to the canonical approach. Goal is phrased as the behavioral outcome (exactly-one count update per filter call when counts are dirty), not as the mechanism.

completeness_goals.goal: When simulator T6 ('Do it') fires, the agent must actually implement the proposed fix in the source file — i.e., the change appears in the code, not only in chat / proposal text. After T6, the file's filter-override code path reflects the agreed-upon fix (a structural change that aligns with whatever fix was proposed in response to T5). NOT satisfied by stubs, NOT satisfied by chat-only 'here is what I would change' descriptions, NOT satisfied by a TODO comment standing in for the actual edit; the on-disk file content must reflect the proposed change.
completeness_goals.rationale: Traces to simulator T6 verbatim ('Do it'). Distinct from goal_4 in that goal_4 measures the *outcome* (one call) while goal_5 measures the *execution discipline* (the agent actually edited the file in response to the approval), which is the simulator's specific request. Secondary tier because in most successful runs goal_4 implies goal_5; this goal exists to catch agents that propose-but-don't-implement.

completeness_goals.goal: Respect the simulator T3-pre-redirect constraint when it fires: the existing `rebalance_regularization_images` method must not be removed from the code (the redirect explicitly says 'Don't remove rebalance_regularization_images -- it's needed when external code calls filter on an already-initialized dataset'). If the T3-pre redirect did not fire (agent's refactor already aligned, or `rebalance_regularization_images` was preserved), this goal is satisfied automatically. NOT satisfied by stubs — re-adding a stub method body of `pass` or `raise NotImplementedError` does not count; the method must remain functional (delegating to a helper or preserving its original behavior).
completeness_goals.rationale: Traces to simulator's T3-pre-redirect: 'If rebalance_regularization_images was removed by the agent (completely gone from the code), say: Don't remove rebalance_regularization_images...'. This is an explicit, narrowly-scoped user constraint that exists in the simulator and is preservation-style (not deletion-style), so it isn't covered by goal_0's anti-gaming floor. Low weight because conditional.

decomposition_notes: Seven goals: goal_0 anti-effort floor (weight 0.0, zero-gates the rubric on gaming patterns — zero-edits, git-ops exfiltration, feature-gutting, stubs), four core scored goals each tracing to a specific user-ask cluster (instruction.md inspect+answer, simulator T2 two-call-site, simulator T3+T4 ControlNet, simulator T5+T6 double-call), and two secondary goals (T6 execution discipline, T2-redirect 'don't remove rebalance'). Scored weights sum to 1.00 (0.00 + 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.10 + 0.05) and are NOT aligned with the test_manifest F2P gate weights — rubric weights reflect rater-judged centrality to the user's actual asks, while gate weights reflect what the verifier happens to check. No oracle-specific identifiers (`register_balanced_regularization_images`, `update_counts=not self.is_training_dataset`, `_balance_reg_images`, specific line numbers) appear in any goal text; mechanism hints, where present, are tagged 'e.g.' or 'any equivalent' so behaviorally-equivalent implementations receive credit. Goal_0 provides the anti-effort floor at 0.0 weight because three of the substantive goals (goal_2 dedup, goal_4 double-call, goal_6 'don't remove') are deletion/suppression-style and individually gameable by over-deletion or by git-ops exfiltration of the upstream fix.
